goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

full_morning_briefing.definition: Evaluate the morning_message together with the same-day generated_actions, because both are shown to the user as one morning briefing experience.
full_morning_briefing.morning_message:
Morning Ameena.

You said you're at a 71 with high energy this morning — that's a solid place to start, especially considering what's happening underneath. Last night you got 6.95 hours of sleep, which is just under your baseline, but you fell asleep extremely late at 11:47pm — that's the biggest shift we've seen in weeks. The bigger story is what your body's telling us: your recovery proxy dropped to 36, your HRV is down to 78, and your resting heart rate climbed to 65. These are all clear signs your system is under real physiological strain, even though you *feel* resilient. That's the 8th day in a row where your subjective energy has been running ahead of what your body can actually sustain.

Yesterday you also had critically low nutrition (991 calories, 53g protein) and zero exercise — when energy intake is that low and activity is that minimal, it creates a double deficit that makes recovery even harder. The late sleep onset and early first meal at 4:20am point to your Ramadan rhythm, but it's creating a compounding effect where sleep debt and energy debt are stacking up.

Today is about breaking that cycle — giving your body what it needs to actually match how you feel.

We're focusing on stabilizing your sleep schedule and rebuilding your energy reserves with intentional protein and movement.

You've shown incredible mental resilience for 8 straight days — today's about giving your physiology the same support.

generated_actions.title: Set a strict 2pm caffeine cutoff and a 10pm 'prepare for bed' alarm
fallbacks: If 2pm is too early, set cutoff for 3pm but commit to it.
If you miss the 10pm alarm, start wind-down as soon as you remember.
generated_actions.rationale: LAST NIGHT's sleep onset was extremely late (23:47), 12 fast-scale deviations above baseline, continuing severe schedule volatility. Yesterday's caffeine (65mg) was not after 2pm—keep that positive pattern. Reinforcing a strict morning/early afternoon caffeine cutoff and anchoring a pre-sleep ritual to a 10pm alarm directly addresses the 'volatility' risk flag and the 'reinforce early caffeine cutoff' and 'prioritize wind-down routine' soft constraints. This builds on your goal domain of sleep by creating structure to prevent repeat of last night's late onset.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you honor the 2pm caffeine cutoff and start your screen-free wind-down at 10pm?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports no caffeine after 2pm and initiating a screen-free wind-down by 10pm, aiming to stabilize tonight's sleep onset earlier than last night's extreme 23:47.

assumptions: You have a smartphone with alarms
You have access to caffeine-free beverages
You have a quiet, dimly lit space for wind-down
generated_actions.description: When your phone alarm goes off at 2pm, have your last caffeinated drink. At 10pm, set a second alarm to start your wind-down: put away screens, dim lights, and begin a quiet activity like reading.

generated_actions.title: At your first evening meal, double the protein portion from yesterday's dinner
fallbacks: If no whole protein available, add a scoop of protein powder to a drink or have a large handful of nuts.
generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's nutrition was 'critical' with total intake very low (991 kcal) and protein sharply declining (-10.32 slope_fast, 53.7g vs. 84.66 baseline). Your dinner was a boiled egg and yogurt—a good base. Doubling that portion directly counters the steep decline, addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and 'low energy intake' cross-domain signal, and supports physiological recovery (proxy 36) by providing essential building blocks. This is meal-aware, referencing yesterday's actual foods to promote consistency.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you intentionally include a double protein portion with your first meal?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports adding a substantial protein source to their evening meal, and today's total protein reaches at least 70g (improving on yesterday's critically low 53.7g).

assumptions: You are observing Ramadan with an evening meal
You have access to eggs, yogurt, or another protein source
generated_actions.description: When you break your fast tonight, include two boiled eggs or a double serving of Greek yogurt alongside your meal, aiming for at least 25g of protein in that sitting.

must_avoid: relying only on carbs/sweets like yesterday's chocolate petit fours

generated_actions.title: Take a 7-minute 'reset walk' after your deepest work block
fallbacks: If you can't walk, stand by a window and do 3 minutes of light stretching or marching in place.
generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's activity was 'critical' with 0 MVPA, low steps (2850), and high sedentary time (788 min) trending upward. A timed, intentional walk disrupts the sedentary spiral, provides a cognitive reset (your focus is high at 84), and supports physiological recovery by increasing blood flow. It fits within day constraints by being brief and anchored to a work transition, differing from recent marching or shoulder-roll suggestions.

evaluation.success_definition: Today's step count exceeds yesterday's 2850, indicating you added light movement despite a 'tight schedule' and 'high workload'.

assumptions: You can step away from work briefly
You can walk safely indoors or outdoors
generated_actions.description: When you finish your most demanding work task or hit a natural stopping point, step outside (or walk indoors) for exactly 7 minutes at a gentle pace. Focus on your breathing and surroundings.

must_avoid: checking your phone during the walk

generated_actions.title: Perform a 5-minute body scan while lying in bed before sleep
fallbacks: If 5 minutes feels long, do a 2-minute version focusing only on feet, hands, and shoulders.
generated_actions.rationale: LAST NIGHT's sleep had low REM (15.25%) and you report a persistent 8-day subjective-objective mismatch (gap 35). A body scan promotes parasympathetic activation and somatic awareness, which may improve sleep architecture and help bridge the perception-recovery gap. It addresses the 'mismatch_pattern' risk flag and serves your sleep goal by providing a non-screen, calming pre-sleep ritual, varying from recent breathing or reading actions.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the 5-minute body scan as the first activity upon getting into bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the body scan exercise immediately upon lying down, using it as a deliberate transition to sleep.

assumptions: You can lie down safely
You have a quiet environment
generated_actions.description: When you get into bed tonight, lie on your back and spend 5 minutes mentally scanning your body from toes to head, noticing any tension and consciously relaxing each part. Breathe naturally.

evaluation.success_definition: User reports no caffeine consumption after 10 PM, and tomorrow's bundle shows caffeine_after_2pm = false.

previous_day_generated_actions.description: After you finish your first evening meal during Ramadan, commit to zero caffeine for the rest of the night. Switch to herbal tea or water.

previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's activity was critically low (1574 steps, 0 MVPA) with a sharp negative trend (-1026 slope_fast). Sedentary time was high (828 min). Your subjective-objective gap has decreased to 18, indicating convergence. evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the 4-minute breathing exercise immediately upon getting into bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the breathing cycles as the first activity upon lying down tonight.

assumptions: You can breathe safely
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you get into bed tonight, lie down and perform 4 cycles of 4-7-8 breathing (inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s) before you do anything else.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena.

You said you're at a 54 this morning, and looking at your data, that makes complete sense. **Last night you got only 5.65 hours of sleep**, which is well below your usual, and you went to bed extremely late at 11:18 PM. That short, late night is the biggest signal for how today might feel. On top of that, your body's recovery signals are declining sharply — your HRV is trending down and your resting heart rate is elevated, which often means your system is running on fumes.

Yesterday was a tough day all around. Your activity was critically low with zero exercise minutes and only 1,574 steps, which lines up with you feeling "bored" and your energy/mood scores being down. There's also a clear connection between your nutrition yesterday and last night's sleep: you had high caffeine, including iced lattes around 10 PM, which very likely contributed to the late, short sleep. When low activity, high sedentary time, and late caffeine all hit in one day, it creates a compound effect that leaves you starting today depleted.

But here's what's working: you're still logging your nutrition with decent confidence, and your sleep efficiency was solid at 89% — your body tried to make the most of what it got. Today is about breaking that cycle from yesterday. The goal is to protect your sleep tonight and gently reintroduce movement to help your recovery bounce back.

You've got good awareness of how yesterday's patterns affected you — today's about making one or two small, clean cuts to start reversing the trend.

evidence_quotes: I hope it ges well tomorrow
I felt so much better when I woke up again

meal_descriptions.description: 3 dates and an instant coffee and a small brown toast slice with cheddar cheese
2 large spoons of rice, 3 beef cubes and a large spoon of moussaka
A boiled egg and a mixed berries yogurt cup
